President Donald Trump spent the bulk of his speech at the NATO summit airing frustrations about Iran, telling allies that the US isn’t interested in regime change anymore and that European countries have let him down repeatedly.
According to people who heard his closed-door address, Trump complained about the lack of support the US has had from European partners in his war with the Islamic Republic. He also renewed warnings about what he described as the continent’s migration problem, said the people…
European officials had wanted to shift Trump’s focus to Ukraine, but in his speech they got little more than a generic promise of support. Trump told his allies that the US is willing to continue to sell weapons to Europeans, who can then hand them over to Kyiv.
In a briefing with reporters last week, a senior US official said that neither Ukraine nor Russia is making much progress in the war and that Trump urgently wants to end the fighting.
The views the US president set out in the private session weren’t much different from what the president has said in public. but the tone was less belligerent, the people said. Notably, the president didn’t bring up Greenland or Spain — two topics which prompted tirades during a pool spray.
Nor did the president mention NATO’s goal for members to spend 5% of GDP on defense by 2035, the people said.
